name: Fractions ↔ Decimals — Two Names, One Number
whatIsIt: Fractions and decimals are TWO LANGUAGES for the same numbers! The translator is equivalent fractions: rename any fraction with 10 or 100 at the bottom, and it becomes a decimal instantly. 1/2 = 5/10 = 0.5; 3/4 = 75/100 = 0.75; 1/5 = 2/10 = 0.2. Same point on the number line, two name tags.
whyNeeded: Life switches languages constantly: the recipe says half a cup, the measuring jug says 0.5! Prices, measurements, and sports stats speak decimal; sharing and cooking speak fraction. A translator moves freely between both — and picks whichever makes each problem easiest.
realLifeUses: Half a litre on a 0.5 L bottle label — same amount, two languages
Quarter kg of sweets = 0.25 kg on the digital scale
Three-quarters of the match done = 0.75 of the time gone

vocabulary.meaning: Rewrite a fraction as a decimal (or back) without changing the amount.
10 and 100 — bottoms that turn straight into decimal seats!

story.title: Two Languages for the Same Amount
story.text: At the grocer's, the weights are marked in halves and quarters. 'Half a kilo — is that a decimal too?' asks Meena. 'Fractions and decimals are two LANGUAGES for the same amount,' says Thatha. '1/2 is 5/10 is 0.5; 3/4 is 75/100 is 0.75; and 0.25 is 25/100, which is 1/4. Translate freely between them!'
story.extractedProblem: Translate 1/2 and 3/4 into decimals, and 0.25 back into a fraction.

visual.caption: The shading lines up: 1/2 and 5/10 and 0.5 are ONE amount. Equivalent fractions are the translator!

teachingGallery.title: Two names, one number
teachingGallery.note: A tenth is 1/10 or 0.1 — same amount, different clothes.

examples.caption: Same point on the line: 7/10 and 0.7.

standardMethod.summary: Fraction → decimal: rename with bottom 10 or 100. Decimal → fraction: read the seats, then simplify.
steps: Fraction → decimal: multiply top and bottom to make the bottom 10 or 100. 1/2 = 5/10 = 0.5; 3/4 = 75/100 = 0.75.

alternateMethods.name: Number-line meeting point
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To PROVE two names are one number.
steps: Draw 0 to 1 cut into 4 jumps; mark 3/4.
Same spot! One home, two name plates.
alternateMethods.example: 1/2 and 0.5 both live exactly at the middle.

alternateMethods.name: Money translation
steps: Fractions of a rupee ARE decimals of a rupee!
Make the bottom 10: multiply top and bottom by 2.

workedExamples.problem: The scale shows 0.75 kg of sweets. Amma asked for three-quarters kg. Did the shopkeeper weigh right?
steps: 0.75 kg IS three-quarters kg — perfectly weighed! Two languages, one amount.

trickPractice.intro: A single digit after the dot is just that many tenths — bottom is 10.

hintLadder: One digit after the dot = TENTHS.
One digit after the dot = TENTHS.
One digit after the dot = TENTHS.
One digit after the dot = TENTHS.
One digit after the dot = TENTHS.
One digit after the dot = TENTHS.

commonMistakes.mistake: Translating 1/2 as 0.12 or 1.2 — copying the fraction's digits into decimal seats.
commonMistakes.fix: Digits don't teleport — AMOUNTS translate! Rename first: 1/2 = 5/10, and 5 tenths = 0.5. Always route through a friendly bottom (10 or 100).

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing 1/4 as 0.4 ('the 4 goes after the dot').
commonMistakes.fix: 1/4 = 25/100 = 0.25! Money proof: a quarter of a rupee is 25 paise, not 40. The famous pairs are worth knowing cold.

commonMistakes.mistake: Leaving 0.25 translated as 25/100 and stopping there.
commonMistakes.fix: Finish the translation with a simplify: 25/100 ÷25 = 1/4 — the simplest name is the most useful one (and the one recipes use!).

hintLadder: One seat after the dot = tenths.
Money proof: a quarter of a rupee = ? paise.

challenge.q: The recipe needs 1/5 cup of oil; your measuring cup shows decimals. How much do you pour?

options.label: They mark the SAME point on the number line — one amount, two names

questions.answer: They mark the SAME point on the number line — one amount, two names
hintLadder: Where does each live between 0 and 1?

teachBackPrompt: Make a two-language menu card for your family: pick 3 amounts (half a glass, quarter kg, three-quarters of an hour) and write each in BOTH languages, explaining the translator trick for one of them.

revisionCard.summary: Fractions ↔ decimals = two languages, ONE number. Translator = equivalent fractions via bottoms of 10/100. Famous pairs: 1/2=0.5, 1/4=0.25, 3/4=0.75, 1/5=0.2, 1/10=0.1. Decimal→fraction: read the seats, then SIMPLIFY. Money proves everything!

gameMission.title: The Two-Language Menu
gameMission.brief: Café orders arrive in both languages and the kitchen must match them! Translate fractions to decimals and back before the food gets cold — and catch Ravi serving 0.4 pizza to the 1/4-pizza customers.

realLifeProject: Kitchen translator: find 3 real items with decimal labels (0.5 L bottle, 0.25 kg pack…). Say each in fraction language. Then reverse: measure half a cup of rice and write it as a decimal.
